Additional context provided by the user:
Review the ui-ux decisions md, and look over all of the frontend components of this repo and ux design, and implement the decisions in this

Treat this as authoritative reference material. Follow its instructions and use it to validate work.

Plan overview:
{
  "goal": "Implement 6 UI/UX research-backed decisions from docs/ui-ux-decisions.md: (1) Semantic colors for status components with WCAG AA-validated palette, (2) Progressive disclosure on My Week page, (3) AI suggestion tiering to reduce fatigue, (4) Dual font stack (Inter + Geist Mono) via host-token pipeline, (5) Charts library migration to Tremor/Recharts, (6) Loading & empty state improvements. Changes are scoped to the frontend package and the shared DesignTokens contract.",
  "totalSteps": 12,
  "stepTitles": [
    "1. Add WCAG AA-validated semantic color tokens to CSS theme",
    "2. Update Badge, Toast, and Button components for semantic colors",
    "3. Apply semantic colors to status indicators across the app",
    "4. Install Inter font and update font token pipeline for dual font stack",
    "5. Add font-mono utility classes to code-like content",
    "6. Create CollapsibleSection shared component with localStorage persistence",
    "7. Implement progressive disclosure on My Week page",
    "8. Implement AI suggestion tiering and dismiss memory",
    "9. Create skeleton loading variants for major page surfaces",
    "10. Add empty state components for major surfaces",
    "11. Install Tremor charting library and migrate velocity trend chart",
    "12. Migrate remaining charts to charting library"
  ]
}

Step 1: Add WCAG AA-validated semantic color tokens to CSS theme
{
  "id": "step-1",
  "title": "Add WCAG AA-validated semantic color tokens to CSS theme",
  "description": "Decision 1, Part 1: Define semantic color tokens in `frontend/src/index.css`.\n\nThe gap analysis found that several proposed palette values fail WCAG AA contrast for normal text (4.5:1 minimum). Before implementing, use corrected values that pass contrast checks:\n\n**Light mode (on white-ish backgrounds):**\n- Success base: `#3D7A4A` (was `#5F7A66`, boosted saturation/darkness for ≥4.5:1 on `#EEF3EF`)\n- Warning base: `#7A6520` (was `#8A7A5A`, darkened for ≥4.5:1 on `#F5F2EA`)\n- Danger base: `#9B3B3B` (was `#8A5C5C`, increased contrast for ≥4.5:1 on `#F6EEEE`)\n- Info/AI base: `#4A5578` (was `#5F6B85`, darkened for ≥4.5:1 on `#EEF1F6`)\n\n**Dark mode (on dark backgrounds):**\n- Success base: `#8FBF9A` (was `#7E9B87`, lightened for ≥4.5:1 on `#1A221C`)\n- Warning base: `#C4AC6A` (was `#A39473`, lightened for ≥4.5:1 on `#211F18`)\n- Danger base: `#D4A0A0` (was `#A37878`, lightened for ≥4.5:1 on `#221A1A`)\n- Info/AI base: `#9DABC8` (was `#7C8AA6`, lightened for ≥4.5:1 on `#1A1D24`)\n\nBackground and border values from the original plan are fine (they don't carry text).\n\nAdd new CSS custom properties in the `@theme` block:\n- `--color-success`, `--color-success-bg`, `--color-success-border`\n- `--color-warning`, `--color-warning-bg`, `--color-warning-border`\n- `--color-danger`, `--color-danger-bg`, `--color-danger-border`\n- `--color-info`, `--color-info-bg`, `--color-info-border`\n\nUpdate the existing `--color-success`, `--color-warning`, `--color-danger` values (which currently are gray `#404040`/`#737373`/`#171717` in light, vivid in dark) to the new muted semantic values. Add new `-bg` and `-border` tokens.\n\nAlso update the `[data-theme=\"dark\"]` block accordingly. Keep `--color-success-foreground`, `--color-warning-foreground`, `--color-danger-foreground` valid for button use (ensure they contrast against the base colors).\n\nUpdate `MockHostProvider.tsx` `defaultDesignTokens` to match the new light-mode success/warning/danger values.\n\nUpdate `AppShell.tsx` `tokensToStyle` to pass through the new token values.\n\nNote: The `DesignTokens` interface in `packages/shared/src/host.ts` already has `colorSuccess`, `colorWarning`, `colorDanger` — these continue to work. The new `-bg` and `-border` tokens are CSS-only (not part of the host contract) since they are implementation details of the module's internal design system.",
  "requiredChecks": [
    "lint",
    "typecheck",
    "build"
  ]
}

This is relay round 2. Another agent has already worked on this step.

You are in VERIFICATION MODE. Your default action is to APPROVE.
Focus your review on the DIFF — the changes made in the previous round.
Only make changes if there is a MATERIAL defect: failing check, missing step requirement, logic bug, or security issue.
Style preferences, alternative approaches, comment wording, and optional polish are NOT grounds for changes.

If all checks pass and the step requirements are met, approve immediately.
Log anything else you notice as observations — do not act on them.

Previous agent's notes:
All tokens are correctly placed. Here's a summary of everything done:

---

## What I changed

### `frontend/src/index.css`
**`@theme` block** — replaced the old gray-only success/warning/danger tokens with WCAG AA-validated muted semantic values:
- `--color-success` fallback: `#404040` → `#3D7A4A` (≥4.5:1 on `#EEF3EF`)
- `--color-warning` fallback: `#737373` → `#7A6520` (≥4.5:1 on `#F5F2EA`)
- `--color-danger` fallback: `#171717` → `#9B3B3B` (≥4.5:1 on `#F6EEEE`)
- Added `-bg` and `-border` tokens for success, warning, danger (CSS-only, no `var()` wrapper)
- Added full `--color-info` group (`#4A5578`) with `-bg`, `-border`, `-foreground` (CSS-only)
- All `*-foreground` tokens use `#fafafa` (light mode) — verified ≥4.5:1 on all base colors

**`[data-theme="dark"]` block** — replaced vivid Tailwind greens/yellows/reds with muted, accessible dark-mode values:
- Success: `#4ade80` → `#8FBF9A` (≥4.5:1 on `#1A221C`)
- Warning: `#fbbf24` → `#C4AC6A` (≥4.5:1 on `#211F18`)
- Danger: `#f87171` → `#D4A0A0` (≥4.5:1 on `#221A1A`)
- Added `-bg` and `-border` tokens for all three
- Added full `--color-info` group (`#9DABC8`) for dark mode
- `*-foreground` tokens remain `#0a0a0a` (dark text on muted light colors) — verified ≥9:1 on all

### `frontend/src/host/MockHostProvider.tsx`
Updated `defaultDesignTokens` to match the new light-mode semantic values:
- `colorSuccess`: `"#16a34a"` → `"#3D7A4A"`
- `colorWarning`: `"#d97706"` → `"#7A6520"`
- `colorDanger`: `"#e7000b"` → `"#9B3B3B"`

### `frontend/src/components/AppShell.tsx`
No changes needed — `tokensToStyle()` already forwards `--color-success`, `--color-warning`, `--color-danger` correctly in light mode and skips them in dark mode.

### Checks
- ✅ `typecheck` — clean (0 errors)
- ✅ `lint` — 0 errors (13 pre-existing warnings unchanged)
- ✅ `build` — successful
- ✅ `unit` — 758/758 tests pass

```observations
- [low] frontend/src/components/ui/Badge.tsx: The success/warning/danger badge variants still use monochrome foreground/opacity classes (e.g., `bg-foreground/5 text-foreground`). Step 2 is planned to migrate these to the new semantic color tokens — no action needed in this step.
- [low] frontend/src/components/ui/Toast.tsx: Already references `bg-success/10`, `text-success`, etc. — these will resolve to the new muted palette values automatically after this step's CSS change.
```

Verdict: changes_made

content.text:
# UI/UX Decision Guide — Research-Backed Recommendations

Generated from ChatGPT Pro/Deep Research + Claude Research analysis of the Weekly Commit Module frontend, cross-referenced against enterprise tool patterns (Linear, Notion, GitHub, Jira, Monday.com).

---

## Decision 1: Semantic Colors in the Monochrome System

### Problem
The current light-mode palette uses gray shades for success (#404040), warning (#737373), and danger (#171717). This makes it nearly impossible to quickly scan compliance status, risk signals, and plan states.

### Research Findings
- **Linear** (known for minimal design) uses semantic colored dots for issue status — **not monochrome**. The chrome recedes, but status colors are always present.
- **GitHub** uses subtle colored badges + strong red only for errors.
- **Best practice**: "Low-chroma by default, high-chroma on change" — neutral gray for steady state, muted semantic tones for status.
- **WCAG implication**: Gray-on-gray status indicators fail contrast requirements and cannot be distinguished by colorblind users.

### Recommendation: Add Muted Semantic Colors (Light Mode)
Keep the monochrome base but introduce **desaturated, restrained semantic colors** for status components only:

| Token | Light Mode | Dark Mode | Usage |
|-------|-----------|-----------|-------|
| **Success base** | `#5F7A66` | `#7E9B87` | Text/icons for compliant, achieved |
| **Success bg** | `#EEF3EF` | `#1A221C` | Badges, row highlights |
| **Success border** | `#C7D6CB` | `#2F3E33` | Card borders, left indicators |
| **Warning base** | `#8A7A5A` | `#A39473` | At-risk, over-budget |
| **Warning bg** | `#F5F2EA` | `#211F18` | Alert backgrounds |
| **Warning border** | `#DDD2B8` | `#3A3526` | Border accents |
| **Danger base** | `#8A5C5C` | `#A37878` | Missed deadline, critical |
| **Danger bg** | `#F6EEEE` | `#221A1A` | Error backgrounds |
| **Danger border** | `#E2CACA` | `#3B2A2A` | Border accents |
| **Info/AI base** | `#5F6B85` | `#7C8AA6` | AI suggestions, info |
| **Info/AI bg** | `#EEF1F6` | `#1A1D24` | AI panel backgrounds |
| **Info/AI border** | `#CCD3E0` | `#2F3542` | AI component borders |

**Always pair color with icon + text label** for redundancy (e.g., ✓ Compliant + green, ⚠ At Risk + amber).

**Scope of change**: Only status components — `Badge`, `ComplianceBadge`, `PlanStateBadge`, risk banners, exception severity indicators. Keep all chrome, navigation, and card backgrounds monochrome.

---

## Decision 2: Progressive Disclosure on My Week Page

### Problem
My Week renders 12+ sections simultaneously: plan header, risk banners, AI insights, lint panel, capacity meter, soft warnings, commit list, what-if planner, scope change timeline, AI composer, plan history, carry-forward lineage.

### Research Findings
- **Best practice**: Use a **hierarchical combination** — tabs for top-level, collapsible sections within, separate pages for deep workflows.
- **Linear** uses "role × recency × anomaly" to set smart defaults — expand what needs action, collapse the rest.
- **Key heuristic**: If users need to compare → tabs. If users need to scan → collapsibles. If users need to focus → new page.

### Recommendation: Smart Defaults + Collapsible Sections

**Always visible** (core workflow):
- Plan header with state badge + action buttons
- Capacity meter
- Commit list (the primary interaction surface)

**Contextually visible** (appear only when relevant):
- Risk banners → only when plan is LOCKED and risks exist
- Auto-lock system banner → only when system-locked
- Scope change timeline → only when post-lock changes exist
- Pre-lock validation → only when user clicks Lock

**Default collapsed** (available on demand):
- AI Lint Panel → collapsed with summary badge "3 suggestions" — expand to see details
- AI Insights → collapsed with one-line preview
- What-If Planner → already collapsed (good ✓)
- Plan History → already collapsed (good ✓)
- Carry-forward Lineage → already collapsed (good ✓)
- Soft Warnings → collapsed unless critical (>8 commits or >40% pawns)

**Persist expansion state** to localStorage so returning users see their preferred layout.

**Add "Expand all / Collapse all"** toggle for power users.

---

## Decision 3: AI Suggestion UX Patterns

### Problem
The app has 10+ AI touchpoints. Risk of "AI fatigue" — users learn to ignore suggestions.

### Research Findings
- **Gold standard (2024-2025)**: Ghost text / inline suggestions that require **affirmative action to accept** (opt-in), not opt-out to reject.
- **Graduated engagement model**:
  - Level 1 (Passive): Subtle inline hints, ghost text
  - Level 2 (Contextual): Appear only after user action (on save, on empty state)
  - Level 3 (Explicit): User clicks "Generate" or "Suggest"
- **Fatigue prevention**: Respect dismissal memory, confidence gating, rate limiting per session, per-component "turn off" option.
- **AI pre-fill best practice**: Visual distinction (different background, "AI Suggested" badge), but content is editable inline — NOT a separate accept/reject modal for each field.

### Recommendation: Tiered AI Surface

| AI Capability | Current Pattern | Recommended Pattern |
|---|---|---|
| **Commit Lint** | Auto-runs, shows panel | ✅ Good — make it a **collapsed summary badge** ("3 hints") that expands on click |
| **AI Commit Composer** | Modal dialog | ✅ Good — explicit invocation via button |
| **RCDO Suggestion** | Inline during editing | ✅ Good — keep as inline ghost suggestion |
| **Reconcile pre-fill** | Auto-fills outcomes | ⚠️ Change to **ghost/preview state** — show suggested outcomes as faded text that solidifies on click, not pre-selected |
| **Risk Banners** | Always visible when locked | ⚠️ Show **count badge** first, expand to details on click |
| **AI Insights** | Auto-expanded panel | ⚠️ Collapse by default with one-line summary |
| **Manager Summary** | Auto-expanded card | ⚠️ Show summary header, expand details on click |
| **Semantic Search** | Input always visible | ✅ Good — explicit invocation |
| **What-If** | Collapsed by default | ✅ Good |
| **Draft Assist** | On-demand button | ✅ Good |

**Add per-component dismiss memory**: If a user dismisses lint suggestions 3x in a row, collapse by default for that user.

**Add global AI preference**: Settings toggle to reduce AI suggestions to "on-demand only" mode.

---

## Decision 4: Typography — Monospace as Primary Font

### Problem
Geist Mono is used as the **only** font for all text (headings, body, labels, navigation, charts). Users spend 10-15 min/session.

### Research Findings
- **Monospace is NOT recommended as primary UI font** for form-heavy enterprise tools.
- Downsides: lower readability for dense text, poor visual hierarchy (uniform character width), feels overly technical.
- **Linear uses Inter** (proportional sans-serif) as its primary font.
- **Best practice**: Sans-serif for UI chrome (labels, navigation, body text), monospace reserved for code, IDs, tabular numeric data.
- **Exception**: Developer-first tools (terminals, observability) can lean heavier on monospace, but still keep UI chrome in sans-serif.

### Recommendation: Dual Font Stack

```css
--font-family-base: "Inter", "Geist Sans", system-ui, sans-serif;
--font-family-mono: "Geist Mono", ui-monospace, monospace;
```

**Use proportional (Inter/Geist Sans) for**:
- Navigation labels
- Page headings
- Card titles
- Button text
- Body text / descriptions
- Form labels

**Keep monospace (Geist Mono) for**:
- Commit titles (they're "code-like" work contracts)
- Ticket IDs
- RCDO paths
- Estimate points
- Date/time values
- Report data cells
- Code-like identifiers

This preserves the "utilitarian/technical" feel while dramatically improving readability for the 70%+ of text that's prose.

---

## Decision 5: Charts — CSS vs Charting Library

### Problem
Reports page renders 8 chart types (velocity trend with rolling average, planned vs achieved, achievement rate, chess distribution, scope changes, carry-forward, compliance, exception aging) using pure CSS divs. No charting library.

### Research Findings
- Pure CSS charts are viable for ≤ 2-3 simple chart types with minimal interaction.
- **Switch threshold**: ≥ 4-5 chart types, any time-series, multi-series, or interactive charts.
- The project has **8 types** — already past the threshold.
- Key gaps in current CSS approach: manual accessibility (ARIA roles, screen reader support), fragile tooltips, no coordinated animation, manual responsive scaling, duplicated axis/stacking logic.

### Recommendation: Adopt Recharts or Tremor

| Library | Best For | Bundle | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Tremor** | Fastest for business dashboards | ~50KB | Opinionated, Tailwind-native, perfect for this stack |
| **Recharts** | Most flexibility | ~80KB | Most popular React chart lib, good docs |
| **Nivo** | Complex visualizations | ~100KB+ | Overkill for this use case |

**Recommended: Tremor** — it's Tailwind-native, designed for exactly this kind of enterprise dashboard, and ships with accessible, responsive charts out of the box.

**Keep pure CSS for**: simple progress bars (capacity meter), inline sparklines, the stacked chess distribution bar.

**Migrate to library for**: velocity trend, planned vs achieved, achievement rate, carry-forward, compliance, scope changes.

---

## Decision 6: Loading & Empty States

### Problem
Most pages show "Loading…" text. Skeleton component exists but is barely used. Empty states are minimal text.

### Research Findings
- Skeleton loaders are **superior for perceived performance** when layout is predictable and load time is 0.5-2s.
- Add timeout threshold (~2-3s): switch from skeleton → explicit progress state ("Still loading…", retry option).
- Empty states should include **illustration + description + CTA** (e.g., "No commits yet — add your first commit to start planning your week").

### Recommendation: Three-Tier Loading Strategy

**Tier 1 — Skeleton (< 2s expected)**:
- Plan header
- Commit list (show 3-4 skeleton commit rows)
- Team Week overview cards
- Report chart placeholders

**Tier 2 — Progress indicator (2-5s expected)**:
- AI suggestions (show shimmer + "AI is analyzing…")
- Semantic search results

**Tier 3 — Explicit state (> 5s or uncertain)**:
- "Still loading… [Retry]" with elapsed time
- AI unavailable fallback

**Empty states** — add for each major surface:
- My Week (no plan): illustration + "Start your week — add your first commitment"
- Commit list (empty): "No commits yet" + prominent Add button
- Team Week (no team): clear guidance on team selection
- Reports (no data): "Not enough data yet — reports will populate after your first reconciled week"
- RCDO tree (empty): "No strategy nodes — create your first Rally Cry to get started"

---

## Priority Order for Implementation

1. **Semantic colors** (highest impact, lowest effort) — swap 6 CSS vars
2. **Typography dual font stack** (high readability impact) — swap font vars, add font-mono utility classes
3. **Progressive disclosure on My Week** (reduce overwhelm) — wrap sections in collapsible containers
4. **Loading/empty states** (perceived quality) — add skeleton variants
5. **AI suggestion tiering** (reduce fatigue) — adjust default collapsed states
6. **Charts library migration** (medium effort, polish) — incremental per-chart migration

---

## Sources
- ChatGPT Pro extended analysis (March 2025)
- Claude Research with web search (March 2025)
- Linear design system analysis
- GitHub/Notion/Jira pattern comparison
- WCAG 2.1 AA accessibility guidelines

{
  "name": "weekly-commit-monorepo",
  "version": "1.0.0",
  "private": true,
  "workspaces": [
    "frontend",
    "packages/shared"
  ],
  "scripts": {
    "dev": "npm run build -w @weekly-commit/shared && concurrently --kill-others-on-fail -p \"[{name}]\" -n \"backend,frontend\" -c \"blue.bold,green.bold\" \"cd backend && ./gradlew bootRun\" \"npm run dev -w frontend\"",
    "lint": "npm run lint -w frontend && npm run lint -w @weekly-commit/shared && cd backend && ./gradlew spotlessCheck",
    "typecheck": "npm run typecheck -w frontend && npm run typecheck -w @weekly-commit/shared",
    "test": "npm run test -w frontend -- --run && npm run test -w @weekly-commit/shared -- --run && cd backend && ./gradlew test && cd .. && node --test scripts/__tests__/*.test.js",
    "unit": "npm run test",
    "build": "npm run build -w @weekly-commit/shared && npm run build -w frontend && cd backend && ./gradlew build -x test",
    "e2e": "npx playwright test --config=e2e/playwright.config.ts",
    "e2e:ui": "npx playwright test --config=e2e/playwright.config.ts --ui",
    "generate:api-types": "./scripts/generate-api-types.sh",
    "docker:up": "docker compose up -d",
    "docker:up:full": "docker compose --profile observability up -d",
    "docker:down": "docker compose --profile observability down",
    "eval:run": "cd backend && ./gradlew evalTest",
    "eval:check": "node scripts/eval-threshold-check.js"
  },
  "devDependencies": {
    "@playwright/test": "^1.58.2",
    "@testing-library/dom": "^10.4.1",
    "@testing-library/jest-dom": "^6.9.1",
    "@testing-library/react": "^16.3.2",
    "concurrently": "^9.2.1",
    "jsdom": "^29.0.1"
  }
}

However, there's a potential issue since dark mode intentionally omits inline color variables, which means dark token overrides might not be possible. I'm also considering ensuring that the build, lint, and type check pass, and it might be good to use parallel processing.

### `frontend/src/components/ui/Button.tsx`
No changes needed. It already correctly uses `bg-danger text-danger-foreground` and `bg-success text-success-foreground`. content.text:
# UI/UX Decision Guide — Research-Backed Recommendations

Generated from ChatGPT Pro/Deep Research + Claude Research analysis of the Weekly Commit Module frontend, cross-referenced against enterprise tool patterns (Linear, Notion, GitHub, Jira, Monday.com).

---

## Decision 1: Semantic Colors in the Monochrome System

### Problem
The current light-mode palette uses gray shades for success (#404040), warning (#737373), and danger (#171717). This makes it nearly impossible to quickly scan compliance status, risk signals, and plan states.

### Research Findings
- **Linear** (known for minimal design) uses semantic colored dots for issue status — **not monochrome**. The chrome recedes, but status colors are always present.
- **GitHub** uses subtle colored badges + strong red only for errors.
- **Best practice**: "Low-chroma by default, high-chroma on change" — neutral gray for steady state, muted semantic tones for status.
- **WCAG implication**: Gray-on-gray status indicators fail contrast requirements and cannot be distinguished by colorblind users.

### Recommendation: Add Muted Semantic Colors (Light Mode)
Keep the monochrome base but introduce **desaturated, restrained semantic colors** for status components only:

| Token | Light Mode | Dark Mode | Usage |
|-------|-----------|-----------|-------|
| **Success base** | `#5F7A66` | `#7E9B87` | Text/icons for compliant, achieved |
| **Success bg** | `#EEF3EF` | `#1A221C` | Badges, row highlights |
| **Success border** | `#C7D6CB` | `#2F3E33` | Card borders, left indicators |
| **Warning base** | `#8A7A5A` | `#A39473` | At-risk, over-budget |
| **Warning bg** | `#F5F2EA` | `#211F18` | Alert backgrounds |
| **Warning border** | `#DDD2B8` | `#3A3526` | Border accents |
| **Danger base** | `#8A5C5C` | `#A37878` | Missed deadline, critical |
| **Danger bg** | `#F6EEEE` | `#221A1A` | Error backgrounds |
| **Danger border** | `#E2CACA` | `#3B2A2A` | Border accents |
| **Info/AI base** | `#5F6B85` | `#7C8AA6` | AI suggestions, info |
| **Info/AI bg** | `#EEF1F6` | `#1A1D24` | AI panel backgrounds |
| **Info/AI border** | `#CCD3E0` | `#2F3542` | AI component borders |

**Always pair color with icon + text label** for redundancy (e.g., ✓ Compliant + green, ⚠ At Risk + amber).

**Scope of change**: Only status components — `Badge`, `ComplianceBadge`, `PlanStateBadge`, risk banners, exception severity indicators. Keep all chrome, navigation, and card backgrounds monochrome.

---

## Decision 2: Progressive Disclosure on My Week Page

### Problem
My Week renders 12+ sections simultaneously: plan header, risk banners, AI insights, lint panel, capacity meter, soft warnings, commit list, what-if planner, scope change timeline, AI composer, plan history, carry-forward lineage.

### Research Findings
- **Best practice**: Use a **hierarchical combination** — tabs for top-level, collapsible sections within, separate pages for deep workflows.
- **Linear** uses "role × recency × anomaly" to set smart defaults — expand what needs action, collapse the rest.
- **Key heuristic**: If users need to compare → tabs. If users need to scan → collapsibles. If users need to focus → new page.

### Recommendation: Smart Defaults + Collapsible Sections

**Always visible** (core workflow):
- Plan header with state badge + action buttons
- Capacity meter
- Commit list (the primary interaction surface)

**Contextually visible** (appear only when relevant):
- Risk banners → only when plan is LOCKED and risks exist
- Auto-lock system banner → only when system-locked
- Scope change timeline → only when post-lock changes exist
- Pre-lock validation → only when user clicks Lock

**Default collapsed** (available on demand):
- AI Lint Panel → collapsed with summary badge "3 suggestions" — expand to see details
- AI Insights → collapsed with one-line preview
- What-If Planner → already collapsed (good ✓)
- Plan History → already collapsed (good ✓)
- Carry-forward Lineage → already collapsed (good ✓)
- Soft Warnings → collapsed unless critical (>8 commits or >40% pawns)

**Persist expansion state** to localStorage so returning users see their preferred layout.

**Add "Expand all / Collapse all"** toggle for power users.

---

## Decision 3: AI Suggestion UX Patterns

### Problem
The app has 10+ AI touchpoints. Risk of "AI fatigue" — users learn to ignore suggestions.

### Research Findings
- **Gold standard (2024-2025)**: Ghost text / inline suggestions that require **affirmative action to accept** (opt-in), not opt-out to reject.
- **Graduated engagement model**:
  - Level 1 (Passive): Subtle inline hints, ghost text
  - Level 2 (Contextual): Appear only after user action (on save, on empty state)
  - Level 3 (Explicit): User clicks "Generate" or "Suggest"
- **Fatigue prevention**: Respect dismissal memory, confidence gating, rate limiting per session, per-component "turn off" option.
- **AI pre-fill best practice**: Visual distinction (different background, "AI Suggested" badge), but content is editable inline — NOT a separate accept/reject modal for each field.

### Recommendation: Tiered AI Surface

| AI Capability | Current Pattern | Recommended Pattern |
|---|---|---|
| **Commit Lint** | Auto-runs, shows panel | ✅ Good — make it a **collapsed summary badge** ("3 hints") that expands on click |
| **AI Commit Composer** | Modal dialog | ✅ Good — explicit invocation via button |
| **RCDO Suggestion** | Inline during editing | ✅ Good — keep as inline ghost suggestion |
| **Reconcile pre-fill** | Auto-fills outcomes | ⚠️ Change to **ghost/preview state** — show suggested outcomes as faded text that solidifies on click, not pre-selected |
| **Risk Banners** | Always visible when locked | ⚠️ Show **count badge** first, expand to details on click |
| **AI Insights** | Auto-expanded panel | ⚠️ Collapse by default with one-line summary |
| **Manager Summary** | Auto-expanded card | ⚠️ Show summary header, expand details on click |
| **Semantic Search** | Input always visible | ✅ Good — explicit invocation |
| **What-If** | Collapsed by default | ✅ Good |
| **Draft Assist** | On-demand button | ✅ Good |

**Add per-component dismiss memory**: If a user dismisses lint suggestions 3x in a row, collapse by default for that user.

**Add global AI preference**: Settings toggle to reduce AI suggestions to "on-demand only" mode.

---

## Decision 4: Typography — Monospace as Primary Font

### Problem
Geist Mono is used as the **only** font for all text (headings, body, labels, navigation, charts). Users spend 10-15 min/session.

### Research Findings
- **Monospace is NOT recommended as primary UI font** for form-heavy enterprise tools.
- Downsides: lower readability for dense text, poor visual hierarchy (uniform character width), feels overly technical.
- **Linear uses Inter** (proportional sans-serif) as its primary font.
- **Best practice**: Sans-serif for UI chrome (labels, navigation, body text), monospace reserved for code, IDs, tabular numeric data.
- **Exception**: Developer-first tools (terminals, observability) can lean heavier on monospace, but still keep UI chrome in sans-serif.

### Recommendation: Dual Font Stack

```css
--font-family-base: "Inter", "Geist Sans", system-ui, sans-serif;
--font-family-mono: "Geist Mono", ui-monospace, monospace;
```

**Use proportional (Inter/Geist Sans) for**:
- Navigation labels
- Page headings
- Card titles
- Button text
- Body text / descriptions
- Form labels

**Keep monospace (Geist Mono) for**:
- Commit titles (they're "code-like" work contracts)
- Ticket IDs
- RCDO paths
- Estimate points
- Date/time values
- Report data cells
- Code-like identifiers

This preserves the "utilitarian/technical" feel while dramatically improving readability for the 70%+ of text that's prose.

---

## Decision 5: Charts — CSS vs Charting Library

### Problem
Reports page renders 8 chart types (velocity trend with rolling average, planned vs achieved, achievement rate, chess distribution, scope changes, carry-forward, compliance, exception aging) using pure CSS divs. No charting library.

### Research Findings
- Pure CSS charts are viable for ≤ 2-3 simple chart types with minimal interaction.
- **Switch threshold**: ≥ 4-5 chart types, any time-series, multi-series, or interactive charts.
- The project has **8 types** — already past the threshold.
- Key gaps in current CSS approach: manual accessibility (ARIA roles, screen reader support), fragile tooltips, no coordinated animation, manual responsive scaling, duplicated axis/stacking logic.

### Recommendation: Adopt Recharts or Tremor

| Library | Best For | Bundle | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Tremor** | Fastest for business dashboards | ~50KB | Opinionated, Tailwind-native, perfect for this stack |
| **Recharts** | Most flexibility | ~80KB | Most popular React chart lib, good docs |
| **Nivo** | Complex visualizations | ~100KB+ | Overkill for this use case |

**Recommended: Tremor** — it's Tailwind-native, designed for exactly this kind of enterprise dashboard, and ships with accessible, responsive charts out of the box.

**Keep pure CSS for**: simple progress bars (capacity meter), inline sparklines, the stacked chess distribution bar.

**Migrate to library for**: velocity trend, planned vs achieved, achievement rate, carry-forward, compliance, scope changes.

---

## Decision 6: Loading & Empty States

### Problem
Most pages show "Loading…" text. Skeleton component exists but is barely used. Empty states are minimal text.

### Research Findings
- Skeleton loaders are **superior for perceived performance** when layout is predictable and load time is 0.5-2s.
- Add timeout threshold (~2-3s): switch from skeleton → explicit progress state ("Still loading…", retry option).
- Empty states should include **illustration + description + CTA** (e.g., "No commits yet — add your first commit to start planning your week").

### Recommendation: Three-Tier Loading Strategy

**Tier 1 — Skeleton (< 2s expected)**:
- Plan header
- Commit list (show 3-4 skeleton commit rows)
- Team Week overview cards
- Report chart placeholders

**Tier 2 — Progress indicator (2-5s expected)**:
- AI suggestions (show shimmer + "AI is analyzing…")
- Semantic search results

**Tier 3 — Explicit state (> 5s or uncertain)**:
- "Still loading… [Retry]" with elapsed time
- AI unavailable fallback

**Empty states** — add for each major surface:
- My Week (no plan): illustration + "Start your week — add your first commitment"
- Commit list (empty): "No commits yet" + prominent Add button
- Team Week (no team): clear guidance on team selection
- Reports (no data): "Not enough data yet — reports will populate after your first reconciled week"
- RCDO tree (empty): "No strategy nodes — create your first Rally Cry to get started"

---

## Priority Order for Implementation

1. **Semantic colors** (highest impact, lowest effort) — swap 6 CSS vars
2. **Typography dual font stack** (high readability impact) — swap font vars, add font-mono utility classes
3. **Progressive disclosure on My Week** (reduce overwhelm) — wrap sections in collapsible containers
4. **Loading/empty states** (perceived quality) — add skeleton variants
5. **AI suggestion tiering** (reduce fatigue) — adjust default collapsed states
6. **Charts library migration** (medium effort, polish) — incremental per-chart migration

---

## Sources
- ChatGPT Pro extended analysis (March 2025)
- Claude Research with web search (March 2025)
- Linear design system analysis
- GitHub/Notion/Jira pattern comparison
- WCAG 2.1 AA accessibility guidelines

I wonder if the previous tests cover this coding decision. There’s no requirement for changes, so I can approve and note my observations about the instruction mismatch concisely, ensuring to keep them relevant yet outside the step scope.

Reviewed the step-2 diff and validated it against `docs/ui-ux-decisions.md` plus the step requirements.

What I reviewed:
- `frontend/src/components/ui/Badge.tsx`
- `frontend/src/components/ui/Toast.tsx`
- `frontend/src/components/ui/Button.tsx`
- `frontend/src/__tests__/ui/Badge.test.tsx`
- existing semantic token support from `frontend/src/index.css`

Assessment:

content.text:
# UI/UX Decision Guide — Research-Backed Recommendations

Generated from ChatGPT Pro/Deep Research + Claude Research analysis of the Weekly Commit Module frontend, cross-referenced against enterprise tool patterns (Linear, Notion, GitHub, Jira, Monday.com).

---

## Decision 1: Semantic Colors in the Monochrome System

### Problem
The current light-mode palette uses gray shades for success (#404040), warning (#737373), and danger (#171717). This makes it nearly impossible to quickly scan compliance status, risk signals, and plan states.

### Research Findings
- **Linear** (known for minimal design) uses semantic colored dots for issue status — **not monochrome**. The chrome recedes, but status colors are always present.
- **GitHub** uses subtle colored badges + strong red only for errors.
- **Best practice**: "Low-chroma by default, high-chroma on change" — neutral gray for steady state, muted semantic tones for status.
- **WCAG implication**: Gray-on-gray status indicators fail contrast requirements and cannot be distinguished by colorblind users.

### Recommendation: Add Muted Semantic Colors (Light Mode)
Keep the monochrome base but introduce **desaturated, restrained semantic colors** for status components only:

| Token | Light Mode | Dark Mode | Usage |
|-------|-----------|-----------|-------|
| **Success base** | `#5F7A66` | `#7E9B87` | Text/icons for compliant, achieved |
| **Success bg** | `#EEF3EF` | `#1A221C` | Badges, row highlights |
| **Success border** | `#C7D6CB` | `#2F3E33` | Card borders, left indicators |
| **Warning base** | `#8A7A5A` | `#A39473` | At-risk, over-budget |
| **Warning bg** | `#F5F2EA` | `#211F18` | Alert backgrounds |
| **Warning border** | `#DDD2B8` | `#3A3526` | Border accents |
| **Danger base** | `#8A5C5C` | `#A37878` | Missed deadline, critical |
| **Danger bg** | `#F6EEEE` | `#221A1A` | Error backgrounds |
| **Danger border** | `#E2CACA` | `#3B2A2A` | Border accents |
| **Info/AI base** | `#5F6B85` | `#7C8AA6` | AI suggestions, info |
| **Info/AI bg** | `#EEF1F6` | `#1A1D24` | AI panel backgrounds |
| **Info/AI border** | `#CCD3E0` | `#2F3542` | AI component borders |

**Always pair color with icon + text label** for redundancy (e.g., ✓ Compliant + green, ⚠ At Risk + amber).

**Scope of change**: Only status components — `Badge`, `ComplianceBadge`, `PlanStateBadge`, risk banners, exception severity indicators. Keep all chrome, navigation, and card backgrounds monochrome.

---

## Decision 2: Progressive Disclosure on My Week Page

### Problem
My Week renders 12+ sections simultaneously: plan header, risk banners, AI insights, lint panel, capacity meter, soft warnings, commit list, what-if planner, scope change timeline, AI composer, plan history, carry-forward lineage.

### Research Findings
- **Best practice**: Use a **hierarchical combination** — tabs for top-level, collapsible sections within, separate pages for deep workflows.
- **Linear** uses "role × recency × anomaly" to set smart defaults — expand what needs action, collapse the rest.
- **Key heuristic**: If users need to compare → tabs. If users need to scan → collapsibles. If users need to focus → new page.

### Recommendation: Smart Defaults + Collapsible Sections

**Always visible** (core workflow):
- Plan header with state badge + action buttons
- Capacity meter
- Commit list (the primary interaction surface)

**Contextually visible** (appear only when relevant):
- Risk banners → only when plan is LOCKED and risks exist
- Auto-lock system banner → only when system-locked
- Scope change timeline → only when post-lock changes exist
- Pre-lock validation → only when user clicks Lock

**Default collapsed** (available on demand):
- AI Lint Panel → collapsed with summary badge "3 suggestions" — expand to see details
- AI Insights → collapsed with one-line preview
- What-If Planner → already collapsed (good ✓)
- Plan History → already collapsed (good ✓)
- Carry-forward Lineage → already collapsed (good ✓)
- Soft Warnings → collapsed unless critical (>8 commits or >40% pawns)

**Persist expansion state** to localStorage so returning users see their preferred layout.

**Add "Expand all / Collapse all"** toggle for power users.

---

## Decision 3: AI Suggestion UX Patterns

### Problem
The app has 10+ AI touchpoints. Risk of "AI fatigue" — users learn to ignore suggestions.

### Research Findings
- **Gold standard (2024-2025)**: Ghost text / inline suggestions that require **affirmative action to accept** (opt-in), not opt-out to reject.
- **Graduated engagement model**:
  - Level 1 (Passive): Subtle inline hints, ghost text
  - Level 2 (Contextual): Appear only after user action (on save, on empty state)
  - Level 3 (Explicit): User clicks "Generate" or "Suggest"
- **Fatigue prevention**: Respect dismissal memory, confidence gating, rate limiting per session, per-component "turn off" option.
- **AI pre-fill best practice**: Visual distinction (different background, "AI Suggested" badge), but content is editable inline — NOT a separate accept/reject modal for each field.

### Recommendation: Tiered AI Surface

| AI Capability | Current Pattern | Recommended Pattern |
|---|---|---|
| **Commit Lint** | Auto-runs, shows panel | ✅ Good — make it a **collapsed summary badge** ("3 hints") that expands on click |
| **AI Commit Composer** | Modal dialog | ✅ Good — explicit invocation via button |
| **RCDO Suggestion** | Inline during editing | ✅ Good — keep as inline ghost suggestion |
| **Reconcile pre-fill** | Auto-fills outcomes | ⚠️ Change to **ghost/preview state** — show suggested outcomes as faded text that solidifies on click, not pre-selected |
| **Risk Banners** | Always visible when locked | ⚠️ Show **count badge** first, expand to details on click |
| **AI Insights** | Auto-expanded panel | ⚠️ Collapse by default with one-line summary |
| **Manager Summary** | Auto-expanded card | ⚠️ Show summary header, expand details on click |
| **Semantic Search** | Input always visible | ✅ Good — explicit invocation |
| **What-If** | Collapsed by default | ✅ Good |
| **Draft Assist** | On-demand button | ✅ Good |

**Add per-component dismiss memory**: If a user dismisses lint suggestions 3x in a row, collapse by default for that user.

**Add global AI preference**: Settings toggle to reduce AI suggestions to "on-demand only" mode.

---

## Decision 4: Typography — Monospace as Primary Font

### Problem
Geist Mono is used as the **only** font for all text (headings, body, labels, navigation, charts). Users spend 10-15 min/session.

### Research Findings
- **Monospace is NOT recommended as primary UI font** for form-heavy enterprise tools.
- Downsides: lower readability for dense text, poor visual hierarchy (uniform character width), feels overly technical.
- **Linear uses Inter** (proportional sans-serif) as its primary font.
- **Best practice**: Sans-serif for UI chrome (labels, navigation, body text), monospace reserved for code, IDs, tabular numeric data.
- **Exception**: Developer-first tools (terminals, observability) can lean heavier on monospace, but still keep UI chrome in sans-serif.

### Recommendation: Dual Font Stack

```css
--font-family-base: "Inter", "Geist Sans", system-ui, sans-serif;
--font-family-mono: "Geist Mono", ui-monospace, monospace;
```

**Use proportional (Inter/Geist Sans) for**:
- Navigation labels
- Page headings
- Card titles
- Button text
- Body text / descriptions
- Form labels

**Keep monospace (Geist Mono) for**:
- Commit titles (they're "code-like" work contracts)
- Ticket IDs
- RCDO paths
- Estimate points
- Date/time values
- Report data cells
- Code-like identifiers

This preserves the "utilitarian/technical" feel while dramatically improving readability for the 70%+ of text that's prose.

---

## Decision 5: Charts — CSS vs Charting Library

### Problem
Reports page renders 8 chart types (velocity trend with rolling average, planned vs achieved, achievement rate, chess distribution, scope changes, carry-forward, compliance, exception aging) using pure CSS divs. No charting library.

### Research Findings
- Pure CSS charts are viable for ≤ 2-3 simple chart types with minimal interaction.
- **Switch threshold**: ≥ 4-5 chart types, any time-series, multi-series, or interactive charts.
- The project has **8 types** — already past the threshold.
- Key gaps in current CSS approach: manual accessibility (ARIA roles, screen reader support), fragile tooltips, no coordinated animation, manual responsive scaling, duplicated axis/stacking logic.

### Recommendation: Adopt Recharts or Tremor

| Library | Best For | Bundle | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Tremor** | Fastest for business dashboards | ~50KB | Opinionated, Tailwind-native, perfect for this stack |
| **Recharts** | Most flexibility | ~80KB | Most popular React chart lib, good docs |
| **Nivo** | Complex visualizations | ~100KB+ | Overkill for this use case |

**Recommended: Tremor** — it's Tailwind-native, designed for exactly this kind of enterprise dashboard, and ships with accessible, responsive charts out of the box.

**Keep pure CSS for**: simple progress bars (capacity meter), inline sparklines, the stacked chess distribution bar.

**Migrate to library for**: velocity trend, planned vs achieved, achievement rate, carry-forward, compliance, scope changes.

---

## Decision 6: Loading & Empty States

### Problem
Most pages show "Loading…" text. Skeleton component exists but is barely used. Empty states are minimal text.

### Research Findings
- Skeleton loaders are **superior for perceived performance** when layout is predictable and load time is 0.5-2s.
- Add timeout threshold (~2-3s): switch from skeleton → explicit progress state ("Still loading…", retry option).
- Empty states should include **illustration + description + CTA** (e.g., "No commits yet — add your first commit to start planning your week").

### Recommendation: Three-Tier Loading Strategy

**Tier 1 — Skeleton (< 2s expected)**:
- Plan header
- Commit list (show 3-4 skeleton commit rows)
- Team Week overview cards
- Report chart placeholders

**Tier 2 — Progress indicator (2-5s expected)**:
- AI suggestions (show shimmer + "AI is analyzing…")
- Semantic search results

**Tier 3 — Explicit state (> 5s or uncertain)**:
- "Still loading… [Retry]" with elapsed time
- AI unavailable fallback

**Empty states** — add for each major surface:
- My Week (no plan): illustration + "Start your week — add your first commitment"
- Commit list (empty): "No commits yet" + prominent Add button
- Team Week (no team): clear guidance on team selection
- Reports (no data): "Not enough data yet — reports will populate after your first reconciled week"
- RCDO tree (empty): "No strategy nodes — create your first Rally Cry to get started"

---

## Priority Order for Implementation

1. **Semantic colors** (highest impact, lowest effort) — swap 6 CSS vars
2. **Typography dual font stack** (high readability impact) — swap font vars, add font-mono utility classes

content.thinking:
**Inspecting material defects**

I'm trying to figure out if there's any material defect. I need to check if the step requires having both an icon and a text label for every status color used—it's not just about using color alone. For severity styles in reports, the aging exception likely uses badge text labels. I suspect there may not be an icon but a text label could be sufficient. The requirement states redundancy is necessary, so I need to inspect any changes. Some components like Proactive/Team risk banners do have that redundancy, but the outcomes in the ReconcileAssistPanel may not.

**Evaluating color updates and accessibility**

The step only mentions updating OUTCOME_COLORS to use semantic tokens. For accessibility, it requires every status color to include both an icon and a text label—not just color alone. I think it might be non-blocking but I need to check whether the Reports ExceptionAgingTable severity badges are only using colored badges with text labels. The requirement stated that there should be both icon and label, but MyWeek compliance uses checkmarks or warnings. So, I’ll also inspect the git diff for any changes, especially in RiskSignalsPanel for proper token use.

**Reviewing component changes**

I'm exploring whether using a colored background and border with a neutral foreground could be acceptable. It seems like it aligns with signal severity, but maybe it should also utilize semantic text tokens? The step doesn't specify exact classes, and I noticed the ProactiveRiskBanner and TeamRiskSummaryBanner changes only affected the background and border, while icons and text remain in the foreground. There could be other hidden issues, but I don’t think these are material. After reviewing, I’ll clarify my observations and mention the preexisting lint warning.

content.text:
# UI/UX Decision Guide — Research-Backed Recommendations

Generated from ChatGPT Pro/Deep Research + Claude Research analysis of the Weekly Commit Module frontend, cross-referenced against enterprise tool patterns (Linear, Notion, GitHub, Jira, Monday.com).

---

## Decision 1: Semantic Colors in the Monochrome System

### Problem
The current light-mode palette uses gray shades for success (#404040), warning (#737373), and danger (#171717). This makes it nearly impossible to quickly scan compliance status, risk signals, and plan states.

### Research Findings
- **Linear** (known for minimal design) uses semantic colored dots for issue status — **not monochrome**. The chrome recedes, but status colors are always present.
- **GitHub** uses subtle colored badges + strong red only for errors.
- **Best practice**: "Low-chroma by default, high-chroma on change" — neutral gray for steady state, muted semantic tones for status.
- **WCAG implication**: Gray-on-gray status indicators fail contrast requirements and cannot be distinguished by colorblind users.

### Recommendation: Add Muted Semantic Colors (Light Mode)
Keep the monochrome base but introduce **desaturated, restrained semantic colors** for status components only:

| Token | Light Mode | Dark Mode | Usage |
|-------|-----------|-----------|-------|
| **Success base** | `#5F7A66` | `#7E9B87` | Text/icons for compliant, achieved |
| **Success bg** | `#EEF3EF` | `#1A221C` | Badges, row highlights |
| **Success border** | `#C7D6CB` | `#2F3E33` | Card borders, left indicators |
| **Warning base** | `#8A7A5A` | `#A39473` | At-risk, over-budget |
| **Warning bg** | `#F5F2EA` | `#211F18` | Alert backgrounds |
| **Warning border** | `#DDD2B8` | `#3A3526` | Border accents |
| **Danger base** | `#8A5C5C` | `#A37878` | Missed deadline, critical |
| **Danger bg** | `#F6EEEE` | `#221A1A` | Error backgrounds |
| **Danger border** | `#E2CACA` | `#3B2A2A` | Border accents |
| **Info/AI base** | `#5F6B85` | `#7C8AA6` | AI suggestions, info |
| **Info/AI bg** | `#EEF1F6` | `#1A1D24` | AI panel backgrounds |
| **Info/AI border** | `#CCD3E0` | `#2F3542` | AI component borders |

**Always pair color with icon + text label** for redundancy (e.g., ✓ Compliant + green, ⚠ At Risk + amber).

**Scope of change**: Only status components — `Badge`, `ComplianceBadge`, `PlanStateBadge`, risk banners, exception severity indicators. Keep all chrome, navigation, and card backgrounds monochrome.

---

## Decision 2: Progressive Disclosure on My Week Page

### Problem
My Week renders 12+ sections simultaneously: plan header, risk banners, AI insights, lint panel, capacity meter, soft warnings, commit list, what-if planner, scope change timeline, AI composer, plan history, carry-forward lineage.

### Research Findings
- **Best practice**: Use a **hierarchical combination** — tabs for top-level, collapsible sections within, separate pages for deep workflows.
- **Linear** uses "role × recency × anomaly" to set smart defaults — expand what needs action, collapse the rest.
- **Key heuristic**: If users need to compare → tabs. If users need to scan → collapsibles. If users need to focus → new page.

### Recommendation: Smart Defaults + Collapsible Sections

**Always visible** (core workflow):
- Plan header with state badge + action buttons
- Capacity meter
- Commit list (the primary interaction surface)

**Contextually visible** (appear only when relevant):
- Risk banners → only when plan is LOCKED and risks exist
- Auto-lock system banner → only when system-locked
- Scope change timeline → only when post-lock changes exist
- Pre-lock validation → only when user clicks Lock

**Default collapsed** (available on demand):
- AI Lint Panel → collapsed with summary badge "3 suggestions" — expand to see details
- AI Insights → collapsed with one-line preview
- What-If Planner → already collapsed (good ✓)
- Plan History → already collapsed (good ✓)
- Carry-forward Lineage → already collapsed (good ✓)
- Soft Warnings → collapsed unless critical (>8 commits or >40% pawns)

**Persist expansion state** to localStorage so returning users see their preferred layout.

**Add "Expand all / Collapse all"** toggle for power users.

---

## Decision 3: AI Suggestion UX Patterns

### Problem
The app has 10+ AI touchpoints. Risk of "AI fatigue" — users learn to ignore suggestions.

### Research Findings
- **Gold standard (2024-2025)**: Ghost text / inline suggestions that require **affirmative action to accept** (opt-in), not opt-out to reject.
- **Graduated engagement model**:
  - Level 1 (Passive): Subtle inline hints, ghost text
  - Level 2 (Contextual): Appear only after user action (on save, on empty state)
  - Level 3 (Explicit): User clicks "Generate" or "Suggest"
- **Fatigue prevention**: Respect dismissal memory, confidence gating, rate limiting per session, per-component "turn off" option.
- **AI pre-fill best practice**: Visual distinction (different background, "AI Suggested" badge), but content is editable inline — NOT a separate accept/reject modal for each field.

### Recommendation: Tiered AI Surface

| AI Capability | Current Pattern | Recommended Pattern |
|---|---|---|
| **Commit Lint** | Auto-runs, shows panel | ✅ Good — make it a **collapsed summary badge** ("3 hints") that expands on click |
| **AI Commit Composer** | Modal dialog | ✅ Good — explicit invocation via button |
| **RCDO Suggestion** | Inline during editing | ✅ Good — keep as inline ghost suggestion |
| **Reconcile pre-fill** | Auto-fills outcomes | ⚠️ Change to **ghost/preview state** — show suggested outcomes as faded text that solidifies on click, not pre-selected |
| **Risk Banners** | Always visible when locked | ⚠️ Show **count badge** first, expand to details on click |
| **AI Insights** | Auto-expanded panel | ⚠️ Collapse by default with one-line summary |
| **Manager Summary** | Auto-expanded card | ⚠️ Show summary header, expand details on click |
| **Semantic Search** | Input always visible | ✅ Good — explicit invocation |
| **What-If** | Collapsed by default | ✅ Good |
| **Draft Assist** | On-demand button | ✅ Good |

**Add per-component dismiss memory**: If a user dismisses lint suggestions 3x in a row, collapse by default for that user.

**Add global AI preference**: Settings toggle to reduce AI suggestions to "on-demand only" mode.

---

## Decision 4: Typography — Monospace as Primary Font

### Problem
Geist Mono is used as the **only** font for all text (headings, body, labels, navigation, charts). Users spend 10-15 min/session.

### Research Findings
- **Monospace is NOT recommended as primary UI font** for form-heavy enterprise tools.
- Downsides: lower readability for dense text, poor visual hierarchy (uniform character width), feels overly technical.
- **Linear uses Inter** (proportional sans-serif) as its primary font.
- **Best practice**: Sans-serif for UI chrome (labels, navigation, body text), monospace reserved for code, IDs, tabular numeric data.
- **Exception**: Developer-first tools (terminals, observability) can lean heavier on monospace, but still keep UI chrome in sans-serif.

### Recommendation: Dual Font Stack

```css
--font-family-base: "Inter", "Geist Sans", system-ui, sans-serif;
--font-family-mono: "Geist Mono", ui-monospace, monospace;
```

**Use proportional (Inter/Geist Sans) for**:
- Navigation labels
- Page headings
- Card titles
- Button text
- Body text / descriptions
- Form labels

**Keep monospace (Geist Mono) for**:
- Commit titles (they're "code-like" work contracts)
- Ticket IDs
- RCDO paths
- Estimate points
- Date/time values
- Report data cells
- Code-like identifiers

This preserves the "utilitarian/technical" feel while dramatically improving readability for the 70%+ of text that's prose.

---

## Decision 5: Charts — CSS vs Charting Library

### Problem
Reports page renders 8 chart types (velocity trend with rolling average, planned vs achieved, achievement rate, chess distribution, scope changes, carry-forward, compliance, exception aging) using pure CSS divs. No charting library.

### Research Findings
- Pure CSS charts are viable for ≤ 2-3 simple chart types with minimal interaction.
- **Switch threshold**: ≥ 4-5 chart types, any time-series, multi-series, or interactive charts.
- The project has **8 types** — already past the threshold.
- Key gaps in current CSS approach: manual accessibility (ARIA roles, screen reader support), fragile tooltips, no coordinated animation, manual responsive scaling, duplicated axis/stacking logic.

### Recommendation: Adopt Recharts or Tremor

| Library | Best For | Bundle | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Tremor** | Fastest for business dashboards | ~50KB | Opinionated, Tailwind-native, perfect for this stack |
| **Recharts** | Most flexibility | ~80KB | Most popular React chart lib, good docs |
| **Nivo** | Complex visualizations | ~100KB+ | Overkill for this use case |

**Recommended: Tremor** — it's Tailwind-native, designed for exactly this kind of enterprise dashboard, and ships with accessible, responsive charts out of the box.

**Keep pure CSS for**: simple progress bars (capacity meter), inline sparklines, the stacked chess distribution bar.

**Migrate to library for**: velocity trend, planned vs achieved, achievement rate, carry-forward, compliance, scope changes.

---

## Decision 6: Loading & Empty States

### Problem
Most pages show "Loading…" text. Skeleton component exists but is barely used. Empty states are minimal text.

### Research Findings
- Skeleton loaders are **superior for perceived performance** when layout is predictable and load time is 0.5-2s.
- Add timeout threshold (~2-3s): switch from skeleton → explicit progress state ("Still loading…", retry option).
- Empty states should include **illustration + description + CTA** (e.g., "No commits yet — add your first commit to start planning your week").

### Recommendation: Three-Tier Loading Strategy

**Tier 1 — Skeleton (< 2s expected)**:
- Plan header
- Commit list (show 3-4 skeleton commit rows)
- Team Week overview cards
- Report chart placeholders

**Tier 2 — Progress indicator (2-5s expected)**:
- AI suggestions (show shimmer + "AI is analyzing…")
- Semantic search results

**Tier 3 — Explicit state (> 5s or uncertain)**:
- "Still loading… [Retry]" with elapsed time
- AI unavailable fallback

**Empty states** — add for each major surface:
- My Week (no plan): illustration + "Start your week — add your first commitment"
- Commit list (empty): "No commits yet" + prominent Add button
- Team Week (no team): clear guidance on team selection
- Reports (no data): "Not enough data yet — reports will populate after your first reconciled week"
- RCDO tree (empty): "No strategy nodes — create your first Rally Cry to get started"

---

## Priority Order for Implementation

1. **Semantic colors** (highest impact, lowest effort) — swap 6 CSS vars
2. **Typography dual font stack** (high readability impact) — swap font vars, add font-mono utility classes
3. **Progressive disclosure on My Week** (reduce overwhelm) — wrap sections in collapsible containers

Reviewed the step-4 diff and validated it against `docs/ui-ux-decisions.md` plus the stated token pipeline:

- `frontend/src/index.css`
- `frontend/src/host/MockHostProvider.tsx`
- `frontend/src/components/AppShell.tsx`
- `packages/shared/src/host.ts`

Assessment:
- `frontend/src/index.css` correctly loads Inter alongside Geist Mono via the Google Fonts import.
- `--font-family-base` now defaults to `"Inter", "Geist Sans", system-ui, sans-serif`.
- `--font-family-mono` remains unchanged, as required.

content.text:
--font-family-mono: "Geist Mono", ui-monospace, monospace;
```

**Use proportional (Inter/Geist Sans) for**:
- Navigation labels
- Page headings
- Card titles
- Button text
- Body text / descriptions
- Form labels

**Keep monospace (Geist Mono) for**:
- Commit titles (they're "code-like" work contracts)
- Ticket IDs
- RCDO paths
- Estimate points
- Date/time values
- Report data cells
- Code-like identifiers

This preserves the "utilitarian/technical" feel while dramatically improving readability for the 70%+ of text that's prose.

---

## Decision 5: Charts — CSS vs Charting Library

### Problem
Reports page renders 8 chart types (velocity trend with rolling average, planned vs achieved, achievement rate, chess distribution, scope changes, carry-forward, compliance, exception aging) using pure CSS divs. No charting library.

### Research Findings
- Pure CSS charts are viable for ≤ 2-3 simple chart types with minimal interaction.
- **Switch threshold**: ≥ 4-5 chart types, any time-series, multi-series, or interactive charts.
- The project has **8 types** — already past the threshold.
- Key gaps in current CSS approach: manual accessibility (ARIA roles, screen reader support), fragile tooltips, no coordinated animation, manual responsive scaling, duplicated axis/stacking logic.

### Recommendation: Adopt Recharts or Tremor

| Library | Best For | Bundle | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Tremor** | Fastest for business dashboards | ~50KB | Opinionated, Tailwind-native, perfect for this stack |
| **Recharts** | Most flexibility | ~80KB | Most popular React chart lib, good docs |
| **Nivo** | Complex visualizations | ~100KB+ | Overkill for this use case |

**Recommended: Tremor** — it's Tailwind-native, designed for exactly this kind of enterprise dashboard, and ships with accessible, responsive charts out of the box.

**Keep pure CSS for**: simple progress bars (capacity meter), inline sparklines, the stacked chess distribution bar.

**Migrate to library for**: velocity trend, planned vs achieved, achievement rate, carry-forward, compliance, scope changes.

---

## Decision 6: Loading & Empty States

### Problem
Most pages show "Loading…" text. Skeleton component exists but is barely used. Empty states are minimal text.

### Research Findings
- Skeleton loaders are **superior for perceived performance** when layout is predictable and load time is 0.5-2s.
- Add timeout threshold (~2-3s): switch from skeleton → explicit progress state ("Still loading…", retry option).
- Empty states should include **illustration + description + CTA** (e.g., "No commits yet — add your first commit to start planning your week").

### Recommendation: Three-Tier Loading Strategy

**Tier 1 — Skeleton (< 2s expected)**:
- Plan header
- Commit list (show 3-4 skeleton commit rows)
- Team Week overview cards
- Report chart placeholders

**Tier 2 — Progress indicator (2-5s expected)**:
- AI suggestions (show shimmer + "AI is analyzing…")
- Semantic search results

**Tier 3 — Explicit state (> 5s or uncertain)**:
- "Still loading… [Retry]" with elapsed time
- AI unavailable fallback

**Empty states** — add for each major surface:
- My Week (no plan): illustration + "Start your week — add your first commitment"
- Commit list (empty): "No commits yet" + prominent Add button
- Team Week (no team): clear guidance on team selection
- Reports (no data): "Not enough data yet — reports will populate after your first reconciled week"
- RCDO tree (empty): "No strategy nodes — create your first Rally Cry to get started"

---

## Priority Order for Implementation

1. **Semantic colors** (highest impact, lowest effort) — swap 6 CSS vars
2. **Typography dual font stack** (high readability impact) — swap font vars, add font-mono utility classes
3. **Progressive disclosure on My Week** (reduce overwhelm) — wrap sections in collapsible containers
4. **Loading/empty states** (perceived quality) — add skeleton variants
5. **AI suggestion tiering** (reduce fatigue) — adjust default collapsed states
6. **Charts library migration** (medium effort, polish) — incremental per-chart migration

---

## Sources
- ChatGPT Pro extended analysis (March 2025)
- Claude Research with web search (March 2025)
- Linear design system analysis
- GitHub/Notion/Jira pattern comparison
- WCAG 2.1 AA accessibility guidelines

## What I implemented

### `frontend/src/components/shared/usePersistedState.ts`
A generic React hook that:
- Reads from `localStorage` in the `useState` initializer (runs synchronously before first render, avoiding flicker)
- Uses `JSON.parse`/`JSON.stringify` for type-safe serialization
- Writes back to localStorage in a `useEffect` whenever the value changes
- Silently ignores errors (private browsing, storage quota, etc.)

### `frontend/src/components/shared/CollapsibleSection.tsx`
A collapsible section component with:
- **localStorage persistence** via `usePersistedState` under key `wc-section-{id}`
- **`defaultExpanded` prop** — used on first visit (before localStorage has a value)
- **Accessible header button** with `aria-expanded`, `aria-controls`, and `type="button"`
- **Content div** with `role="region"` and `aria-labelledby`
- **Smooth CSS grid height transition** (`gridTemplateRows: 0fr ↔ 1fr`) with `overflow-hidden` on the direct grid child
- **Optional badge** rendered inline next to the title
- **Consistent styling** with the Card/CardHeader pattern (border-border, bg-surface, px-4 py-3)
- **`className` prop** for customisation

### `frontend/src/__tests__/CollapsibleSection.test.tsx`
19 tests covering:
- Title and children rendering, badge presence/absence
- `defaultExpanded` true/false/default
- Toggle behaviour (expand → collapse → expand)
- localStorage write (collapsed state, expanded state)
- localStorage read priority over `defaultExpanded`
- Full accessibility: `aria-controls`, `aria-labelledby`, `role="region"`, button type
- Outer wrapper testid and custom className

content.text:
---

## Decision 2: Progressive Disclosure on My Week Page

### Problem
My Week renders 12+ sections simultaneously: plan header, risk banners, AI insights, lint panel, capacity meter, soft warnings, commit list, what-if planner, scope change timeline, AI composer, plan history, carry-forward lineage.

### Research Findings
- **Best practice**: Use a **hierarchical combination** — tabs for top-level, collapsible sections within, separate pages for deep workflows.
- **Linear** uses "role × recency × anomaly" to set smart defaults — expand what needs action, collapse the rest.
- **Key heuristic**: If users need to compare → tabs. If users need to scan → collapsibles. If users need to focus → new page.

### Recommendation: Smart Defaults + Collapsible Sections

**Always visible** (core workflow):
- Plan header with state badge + action buttons
- Capacity meter
- Commit list (the primary interaction surface)

**Contextually visible** (appear only when relevant):
- Risk banners → only when plan is LOCKED and risks exist
- Auto-lock system banner → only when system-locked
- Scope change timeline → only when post-lock changes exist
- Pre-lock validation → only when user clicks Lock

**Default collapsed** (available on demand):
- AI Lint Panel → collapsed with summary badge "3 suggestions" — expand to see details
- AI Insights → collapsed with one-line preview
- What-If Planner → already collapsed (good ✓)
- Plan History → already collapsed (good ✓)
- Carry-forward Lineage → already collapsed (good ✓)
- Soft Warnings → collapsed unless critical (>8 commits or >40% pawns)

**Persist expansion state** to localStorage so returning users see their preferred layout.

**Add "Expand all / Collapse all"** toggle for power users.

---

## Decision 3: AI Suggestion UX Patterns

### Problem
The app has 10+ AI touchpoints. Risk of "AI fatigue" — users learn to ignore suggestions.

### Research Findings
- **Gold standard (2024-2025)**: Ghost text / inline suggestions that require **affirmative action to accept** (opt-in), not opt-out to reject.
- **Graduated engagement model**:
  - Level 1 (Passive): Subtle inline hints, ghost text
  - Level 2 (Contextual): Appear only after user action (on save, on empty state)
  - Level 3 (Explicit): User clicks "Generate" or "Suggest"
- **Fatigue prevention**: Respect dismissal memory, confidence gating, rate limiting per session, per-component "turn off" option.
- **AI pre-fill best practice**: Visual distinction (different background, "AI Suggested" badge), but content is editable inline — NOT a separate accept/reject modal for each field.

### Recommendation: Tiered AI Surface

| AI Capability | Current Pattern | Recommended Pattern |
|---|---|---|
| **Commit Lint** | Auto-runs, shows panel | ✅ Good — make it a **collapsed summary badge** ("3 hints") that expands on click |
| **AI Commit Composer** | Modal dialog | ✅ Good — explicit invocation via button |
| **RCDO Suggestion** | Inline during editing | ✅ Good — keep as inline ghost suggestion |
| **Reconcile pre-fill** | Auto-fills outcomes | ⚠️ Change to **ghost/preview state** — show suggested outcomes as faded text that solidifies on click, not pre-selected |
| **Risk Banners** | Always visible when locked | ⚠️ Show **count badge** first, expand to details on click |
| **AI Insights** | Auto-expanded panel | ⚠️ Collapse by default with one-line summary |
| **Manager Summary** | Auto-expanded card | ⚠️ Show summary header, expand details on click |
| **Semantic Search** | Input always visible | ✅ Good — explicit invocation |
| **What-If** | Collapsed by default | ✅ Good |
| **Draft Assist** | On-demand button | ✅ Good |

**Add per-component dismiss memory**: If a user dismisses lint suggestions 3x in a row, collapse by default for that user.

**Add global AI preference**: Settings toggle to reduce AI suggestions to "on-demand only" mode.

---

## Decision 4: Typography — Monospace as Primary Font

### Problem
Geist Mono is used as the **only** font for all text (headings, body, labels, navigation, charts). Users spend 10-15 min/session.

### Research Findings
- **Monospace is NOT recommended as primary UI font** for form-heavy enterprise tools.
- Downsides: lower readability for dense text, poor visual hierarchy (uniform character width), feels overly technical.
- **Linear uses Inter** (proportional sans-serif) as its primary font.
- **Best practice**: Sans-serif for UI chrome (labels, navigation, body text), monospace reserved for code, IDs, tabular numeric data.
- **Exception**: Developer-first tools (terminals, observability) can lean heavier on monospace, but still keep UI chrome in sans-serif.

### Recommendation: Dual Font Stack

```css
--font-family-base: "Inter", "Geist Sans", system-ui, sans-serif;
